g.data-narration: Binary Tree Traversals are fundamental algorithms for visiting all nodes exactly once in a specific order.
Traversal means visiting all nodes of a tree exactly once. There are two main categories: DFS and BFS traversals.
g.text: Visiting all nodes of a tree exactly once. Two categories:

g.data-narration: Let's use this sample binary tree with root value 1, left child 2, right child 3, and 4, 5 as children of 2.
Inorder traversal visits: Left, then Node, then Right. For BSTs, this produces sorted output automatically.
The path traces from left subtree to root to right subtree. Notice how 1 (root) comes between 2's subtree and 3.
Preorder visits Node first, then Left subtree, then Right subtree. Root appears first in the output sequence.
Preorder visits root first, making it perfect for creating a copy of the tree or serializing data structures.
Postorder visits Left, Right, then Node. The root appears last, which is essential for safe tree deletion.
Postorder visits children before parents. This bottom-up approach is crucial for memory deallocation and postfix evaluation.
Level Order visits nodes level by level, from top to bottom, left to right. It uses a Queue data structure for implementation.
g.text: Order: Level by Level, Left → Right

g.data-narration: Level order processes the tree horizontally, layer by layer. Level 1 has node 1, Level 2 has 2 and 3, Level 3 has 4 and 5.
Iterative inorder uses an explicit Stack to simulate recursion. We go left as far as possible, then visit and go right.
Iterative preorder pushes right child first, then left child. This ensures left subtree is processed before right subtree.
This table summarizes all traversals: their order, use cases, and data structures needed for implementation.
All traversals have O(n) time complexity, visiting each node once. Space complexity depends on tree height for DFS and width for BFS.
Key takeaways: DFS uses Stack with three variants (Inorder, Preorder, Postorder). BFS uses Queue for Level Order. Each has unique use cases.
Remember: Inorder for sorted output, Preorder to copy trees, Postorder to delete trees, Level Order for shortest path finding.
Exam tip: Inorder of BST always gives sorted sequence. Preorder root first, Postorder root last. BFS visits level by level.
Master tree traversals to excel in algorithms. Practice implementing both recursive and iterative versions for all four traversal types.
